data.title: Lazard Emerging Markets Equity Advantage Portfolio Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Executive Summary

  • During the quarter, the Emerging Markets Equity Advantage Portfolio outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (net of fees), as stock selection and sector positioning both contributed to the excess return.
  • Stock selection was strongest in the information technology and energy sectors. Consumer staples and real estate holdings lagged.
  • Within sector positioning, the overweight exposure to information technology and underweight exposure in communication services names proved beneficial, although the underweight exposure to financials stocks detracted value.
  • From a country perspective, stock selection was strongest in India and Taiwan and weakest in Korea and Thailand.
  • Alpha was favorable in the quarter, driven by strong results in January and February. Value measures were favorable in every month and the largest source of excess return. Sentiment also contributed, despite a drawdown in March coinciding with the market reversal. Overall, quality barely lagged for the period, helped by a strong February result. Growth measures were weak, underperforming in each month.

Market Review

Emerging equity markets, as measured by the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, ended the quarter broadly flat, declining 0.2%, following a sharp 13.1% selloff in March, the worst monthly decline since the onset of the global pandemic in March 2020. Strength in the US dollar and higher global interest rates contributed to the downturn. Markets had started the quarter strongly, but sentiment deteriorated after the outbreak of the conflict with Iran and the resulting disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Brent crude saw its largest monthly increase on record, raising concerns that elevated energy prices could slow global economic activity. The impact was particularly acute for emerging Asian economies, many of which are significant importers of oil from the Middle East.

Within Asia, South Korea remained highly volatile, including a record single-day decline of 12.2% on 4 March. Despite the sharp selloff, the market finished the quarter up more than 19%, the strongest performance among major markets. Corporate governance reforms and heavy retail investor participation continued to support the market. In China, PMI data at quarter-end exceeded expectations, but equities declined nearly 9% as rising energy costs and continued weakness in domestic consumption weighed on sentiment. Structural challenges stemming from the prior real estate overbuilding cycle continued to dampen investor confidence. Indian equities fell more than 18% during the quarter, as the country's technology sector failed to participate in the global AI-driven rally. Weakness in the rupee and rising inflation also reduced the likelihood that the Reserve Bank of India will cut interest rates, which have increased by more than 100 basis points over the past year. Nonetheless, India's GDP growth remains above 6% and is among the strongest in major emerging economies. Taiwan advanced more than 9% for the quarter despite a sharp pullback in March, though its technology sector remained sensitive to higher energy costs.

Latin American markets were among the primary beneficiaries of the global disruption, rising 14.6% during the quarter as the oil- and commodity-heavy exchanges rallied. AI-driven demand for critical minerals such as copper and lithium provided additional support alongside higher oil prices. Brazil was a notable beneficiary, although the country continues to grapple with inflation near 5% and interest rates approaching 15%, alongside rising debt levels. Mexico also delivered positive returns, supported by continued nearshoring trends a strengthening manufacturing activity. The country reported fourth-quarter GDP growth of 3.5%.

Markets in the Middle East produced mixed results during the quarter. Countries located closer to the Iran conflict experienced losses as supply chains and distribution routes were directly affected. Saudi Arabia, which benefits from alternative export routes and infrastructure, rose 9.2% during the quarter. In South Africa, economic conditions remained challenging, as concerns over political corruption and the potential financial bankruptcy of Johannesburg offset gains from higher gold prices.

European emerging markets posted positive returns for the quarter despite the March selloff. Turkey led the region as inflation declined to a four-year low and corporate earnings remained strong. The central bank reiterated its commitment to combating inflation despite political pressure from the government. In Greece, profit-taking following two years of strong gains contributed to a 6.5% decline. Improvements in the country's banking sector and sovereign credit profile supported its upgrade to developed market status.

From a sector perspective, information technology and energy stocks led market performance during the quarter, while communication services and consumer discretionary lagged. Despite the March selloff, risk-oriented factors performed well overall, with higher volatility stocks outperforming. Value factors outperformed, while growth lagged. Sentiment weakened during March but remained positive for the full quarter. Quality factors provided relative resilience during the market decline but underperformed on a full-quarter basis.

What Helped

  • Delta Electronics (DELTY) (2.0% weighting in the Portfolio) continued its strong momentum in the first quarter, rising another 40% despite a 6% dip in March. The company, which supplies cooling systems for NVIDIA (NVDA)'s power shelves, saw annual revenue increase by nearly 38%. It has positioned itself as the leading supplier of liquid-to-liquid cooling distribution units. Delta Electronics enjoys a nearly 35% operating margin, nearly 15 points above its competitor. The company is looking to expand its operations in Thailand and Mexico as well as the United States.
  • Electrical Industries (0.9% weighting in the Portfolio) reported a 57% increase in profit for 2025 and declared a special dividend, which helped the stock to surge. The stock has surged more than 2x in the past year as energy demand, driven in part by AI usage, continues to grow.
  • Samsung Electronics (SSNLF), which has a 6.7% weighting in the Portfolio, declined following the outbreak of conflict in the Middle East, as escalating tensions raised concerns about the stability of key energy supplies, including crude oil and liquefied natural gas. Higher energy costs could weigh on AI data center economics and slow the pace of AI infrastructure buildouts, while semiconductor fabs in Taiwan and South Korea may face additional margin pressure from rising LNG prices.

What Hurt

  • Canara Bank (CNBKY) (0.5% weighting in the Portfolio) dipped after it reported a 25% increase in profit but missed its net interest income and net interest margin targets. It also reported a large increase in other operating expenses, including high technology capex and an accelerated depreciation charge. Projected lower interest rates in India are also expected to reduce the bank's lending margins.
  • Korea Electric Power (KEP) (0.7% weighting in the Portfolio) was adversely impacted by the rising energy prices. As retail electricity tariffs are heavily regulated, the company has limited ability to pass the costs through to its ratepayers, causing the stock to sell off more than 30% in March.
  • Hyundai Motor (HYMTF) (0.1% weighting in the Portfolio) soared nearly 40% in the quarter despite a 38% selloff in March, reflective of the highly volatile Korean market. The limited exposure to the industrial conglomerate hurt the portfolio. The stock ranks poorly in terms of both valuation and growth potential.

Outlook

The escalation of conflict in the Middle East and the resulting closure of the Strait of Hormuz represent the type of geopolitical shock that had the potential to disrupt equity markets in 2026. The immediate surge in oil prices was a predictable response to the effective removal roughly 20% of the global oil supply from the market. Should prices continue to rise as competition intensifies for a constrained resource base, the implications for the global economy could be significant. European and Asian economies appear particularly exposed, increasing the international incentive to restore maritime access through the Strait as quickly as possible.

While Iran currently holds a tactical advantage through its ability to disrupt shipping, we believe sustained external pressure—from regional Gulf states, global trading partners, and Iran's own economic needs—combined with the continued degradation of military and logistical infrastructure by the United States and Israel, could ultimately push the parties toward negotiations that reopen the Strait. The domestic political backdrop in the United States, including midterm elections and limited public support for an extended conflict, also suggests a willingness to compromise. That said, the probability of this outcome remains highly uncertain, and a range of alternative scenarios could result in further escalation.

If the Strait reopens and peace is achieved, the resolution will likely act as a near-term tailwind for equity markets. However, normalization may take time as supply chains adjust and corporations reassess energy security, potentially accelerating investment in diversification of energy inputs and supply resilience.

Beyond the immediate geopolitical developments, productivity gains associated with artificial intelligence continue to support the medium-term earnings outlook, with the potential to stimulate broader consumer demand outside the United States. At the same time, elevated government deficits—and their implications for interest rates—remain a key macro variable. Stronger global growth could partially offset these pressures by supporting fiscal sustainability. Against a backdrop of geopolitical uncertainty and evolving macro risks, we believe diversification remains the most effective portfolio defense. In this environment, disciplined bottom-up security selection is likely to be the most reliable path to identifying attractive opportunities across global markets.

data.title: Abrdn U.S. Small Cap Equity Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

The Fund gained (gross of fees) and outperformed the Russell 2000 Index.

At the stock level, shares of Viavi Solutions (VIAV) benefited from growth in the data-centre ecosystem and contributions from recent acquisitions. Everus Construction Group (ECG)'s shares rose after the company issued above-consensus guidance, supported by growing data-centre demand and solid execution. Magnolia Oil & Gas (MGY)' shares benefited from a rise in the crude oil price, driven by geopolitical supply concerns.

Conversely, Alphatec Holdings (ATEC)' shares sold off after reporting fourth-quarter earnings below elevated investor expectations, alongside heightened scrutiny across high-growth medical device names. Shares of Q2 Holdings (QTWO) fell amid continued investor concerns regarding potential artificial intelligence-driven disruption. ADMA Biologics (ADMA)' shares declined after management failed to directly address key concerns on revenue quality, leading to sustained pressure on the stock.

Market review

For the first quarter, US small caps gained marginally and outperformed broader US equities, which fell over the period. US equities were relatively resilient earlier in the quarter. However, this strength gave way to a sharp downturn toward quarter end due to a volatile March. Uncertainty over tariffs and softer economic data contributed to weaker sentiment, while the Iran war was the main driver of the March sell-off.

Military strikes involving Israel and the US against Iran contributed to a sharp escalation in regional tensions. Iran subsequently launched retaliatory strikes across the region, leading to airspace closures and the blockage of most oil shipments via the Strait of Hormuz. Crude oil prices rose sharply, raising concerns over oil supply and renewed inflationary pressures.

US President Donald Trump opted not to impose tariffs on European countries in relation to the Greenland dispute. In February, the Supreme Court revoked a large portion of the US administration's existing trade tariffs, prompting President Trump to impose a 10% global tariff as a replacement. Subsequent proposals to raise this rate to 15% contributed to renewed uncertainty around the global trade outlook.

During the quarter, the US Federal Reserve (Fed) kept the federal funds target range at 3.50%–3.75%. Fed Chair Jerome Powell reiterated the central bank's commitment to its 2% inflation goal, as measured by the core Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index. The Fed's latest 'dot plot' showed that policymakers expected to cut rates only once in 2026, while investors expected rates to remain on hold. President Trump also nominated former Fed governor Kevin Warsh to become Fed Chair from May 2026, pending Senate approval.

Activity

We initiated several positions. Garrett Motion (GTX) is a leader in the global turbocharger market. We expect the company to benefit from diversification into industrial applications such as heating, ventilation and air conditioning, and data-centre cooling.

Mirion Technologies (MIR) is a global leader in radiation detection and measurement. We believe Mirion is well positioned to benefit from the accelerating 'nuclear renaissance' as global capacity expands and the installed base continues to age.

Patrick Industries (PATK) is a leading component solutions supplier for original equipment manufacturers across recreational vehicle, marine and manufactured housing industries. We think the company is well positioned for volume normalisation as demand improves.

Quaker Chemical Corporation (KWR) is a global pure-play in industrial process fluids with a highly sticky, service-driven business model. Strong new-business wins and early benefits from cost-reduction initiatives have supported operating momentum. We expect this strength to continue as the company streamlines its network and expands in higher-growth regions.

Steven Madden (SHOO), a leading designer and marketer of footwear and accessories. We believe the company is well-positioned to benefit from consumer discretionary spending, which continues to support demand across its core brands.

Conversely, we sold our positions in ADMA Biologics, Corcept Therapeutics (CORT), SM Energy (SM), Vertex (VERX) and Workiva (WK).

Outlook & strategy

March was defined by sweeping changes in global trade dynamics and a shift in market focus from speculative policy outcomes to the tangible economic consequences of geopolitical conflict. Escalating tensions in the Middle East have pushed energy prices sharply higher, driving renewed concerns over supply-driven inflation at a time when the labour market is beginning to soften. These pressures threaten to temper the growth expected to emerge from the US Congress' 'One Big Beautiful Bill Act' and have revived stagflation concerns. Together, these developments have created a more complex and fragile macroeconomic backdrop for both policymakers and investors.

These pressures were reflected in recent market behaviour, where factor-driven movements have, at times, outweighed company-specific fundamentals. While this environment can pose short-term challenges for active managers, it also creates opportunities for disciplined, fundamental investors to identify and capitalise on valuation dislocations. We believe these conditions are particularly supportive for active management within domestic small- and mid-cap equities, where inefficiencies tend to be more pronounced. Accordingly, we remain optimistic about the long-term outlook for this asset class, which we believe is well positioned to benefit from secular trends such as increased infrastructure investment, accelerating technological innovation and the reshoring of supply chains. The strategy's emphasis on higher-quality businesses provides additional resilience, helping to mitigate macroeconomic headwinds while supporting sustainable growth. In this context, we believe patience, selectivity and a long-term focus remain critical as market volatility creates opportunities for differentiated outcomes over the cycle.
